headline: Trump Confirms Opening of Hormuz and Says Iran Deal Is Close. Oil Settles Below $90.
summary: President Donald Trump said Friday afternoon that the Strait of Hormuz is fully open and “ready for business,” confirming what Iran had declared hours earlier.  About 9 a.m. Eastern time, Iran said the waterway was open to commercial traffic for the duration of the cease-fire between Israel and Lebanon.  Minutes later, Trump thanked Iran in a post on Truth Social.

Minutes later, Trump thanked Iran in a post on Truth Social.

headline: How The Exxon Mobil (XOM) Narrative Is Shifting With New Oil Price And Valuation Assumptions
summary: Exxon Mobil’s updated analyst fair value estimate has shifted from US$160.17 to US$164.13, signaling a modest reset in where research models place the shares. Across recent reports, this adjustment sits within a wider debate over how much war related supply risk, revised long run oil price assumptions, and changed cash flow outlooks are already built into the current share price. Read on to see how to track these evolving targets and what they might mean for your own view on Exxon Mobil. Stay...

headline: Oil Settles Below $90 as Middle East Tensions Ease. Here Is the Road Map to $70 a Barrel.
summary: The price of U.S. oil settled below $90 for the first time since late March after Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz was fully open to commercial traffic for the duration of the cease-fire between Israel and Lebanon.  WTI futures have fallen 19% in April but remain up 44% so far this year.  After the initial excitement over the strait’s reopening, several analysts are striking a note of caution over the market euphoria.

After the initial excitement over the strait’s reopening, several analysts are striking a note of caution over the market euphoria.

headline: Oil Prices Sink on Strait of Hormuz Reopening. What’s in the ‘Fine Print.’
summary: Oil prices were plunging Friday as Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz fully open to commercial traffic for the duration of the cease-fire between Israel and Lebanon.  Brent crude futures  the international benchmark, fell 10.5% to $88.92 a barrel, while  West Texas Intermediate  futures slipped 11.1% to $81.08—on track to settle below $90 for the first time since March 23 and on pace for its lowest close since March 10.  “The fine print is important,” RBC Capital Markets’ Helima Croft told Barron’s. “While the market continues to price a full resumption of flows, a number of shippers/insurers may be reluctant to rush back in with Iran having a final say on passage.”

raw.headline: Oil Prices Sink on Strait of Hormuz Reopening. What’s in the ‘Fine Print.’
raw.summary: Oil prices were plunging Friday as Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz fully open to commercial traffic for the duration of the cease-fire between Israel and Lebanon.  Brent crude futures  the international benchmark, fell 10.5% to $88.92 a barrel, while  West Texas Intermediate  futures slipped 11.1% to $81.08—on track to settle below $90 for the first time since March 23 and on pace for its lowest close since March 10.  “The fine print is important,” RBC Capital Markets’ Helima Croft told Barron’s. “While the market continues to price a full resumption of flows, a number of shippers/insurers may be reluctant to rush back in with Iran having a final say on passage.”

headline: Energy Stocks Lose Wartime Gains
summary: U.S. energy stocks are falling on reports that the Strait of Hormuz was open to commercial traffic, further erasing gains from the first weeks of the war with Iran. Shares in fuel-refiner Phillips 66 and major oil companies Chevron and Exxon Mobil were all down about 6% in morning trading, which followed announcements from President Trump and the Iranian foreign minister declaring the strait open.

headline: Oil Prices Fall. Trump Says Iran War Going ‘Swimmingly’ and Should End Soon.
summary: Oil prices were falling Friday as a 10-day cease-fire between Israel and Lebanon came into effect and President Donald Trump sounded optimistic about a deal with Iran.  Brent crude futures  the international benchmark, fell 3% to $96.3 a barrel, while  West Texas Intermediate  futures slipped 3.7% to $87.7—on track to settle below $90 for the first time since March 23.  WTI futures have fallen 12% in April but remain 55% up so far this year.

WTI futures have fallen 12% in April but remain 55% up so far this year.
